title: Pressure-cooker red lentil dhal
subtitle: Red lentils with cumin, turmeric, and a tempered ghee finish — twenty minutes from cupboard to bowl.
excerpt: Red lentils cooked under pressure with onion, ginger, and turmeric, then finished with a tempered ghee (tarka) of cumin seeds, garlic, and chilli sizzled in hot fat. The most reliably good vegan dinner in the canon.
sourceNotes: Tarka dhal is a Punjabi staple, eaten at every meal in millions of homes. The technique of tempering (chaunk, tarka, or vagar depending on the region) finishes the dish: whole spices and aromatics fried in hot fat poured over the cooked lentils at the moment of serving. The flavour difference between a tempered and untempered dhal is the difference between a finished and unfinished dish.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to three months. Add the tempered tarka after reheating, not before freezing.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. The dhal base freezes; temper fresh each time you serve it.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in the fridge for four days. Thicken on reheating with a splash of hot water.

glossaryTerms.definition: Whole spices and aromatics fried briefly in hot ghee or oil and poured over a cooked dish at the moment of serving. The Punjabi term; called chaunk in Hindi and vagar in Gujarati. The technique releases volatile aromatic compounds that get lost during long cooking.

content.text: Red lentils (masoor dal in Hindi), split and skinless, collapse to a creamy purée in 12 minutes under pressure. The base dhal is straightforward: lentils, water, salt, turmeric, ginger, onion. The dish gets its character from the
, the tempered fat poured over at the end. Without it, you have soup; with it, you have dhal.

The pressure cooker is the right tool because red lentils foam aggressively in an open pot — the steam pressure keeps the foam contained and turns the lentils to a smooth purée without constant skimming. Twelve minutes at high pressure is the limit; longer and the lentils break down entirely into a slurry rather than a purée with body.

items.prepNote: rinsed in cold water until the water runs clear

content.text: A 6-litre pressure cooker plus a small frying pan for the tarka. Use ghee if you have it; vegetable oil works as a vegan alternative.

Put {{red-lentils}} of rinsed red lentils, {{water}} of hot water, the chopped {{onion}} onion, {{ginger-root}} of grated ginger, {{turmeric}} of turmeric, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt into the pressure cooker. Stir. Lock the lid and cook at high pressure for 12 minutes. Let the pressure release naturally for 10 minutes, then quick-release any remaining pressure.

Open the lid. The lentils should have collapsed into a thick, golden purée. Whisk briefly with a balloon whisk to smooth it out; the texture should be like a thick soup. If too thick, stir in hot water a splash at a time; if too thin, set the cooker back to sauté and reduce for 5 minutes.

Melt {{ghee}} of ghee in a small frying pan over a medium-high heat. When it shimmers, add {{cumin-seeds}} of cumin seeds and {{mustard-seeds-black}} of black mustard seeds. They should crackle within seconds. Add {{garlic}} of sliced garlic and {{chilli-red}} of sliced red chilli; fry for one minute until the garlic turns golden. Add {{asafoetida}} of asafoetida. The whole tarka takes 90 seconds; longer and the garlic burns and the dhal tastes bitter.

Pour the tarka (fat, seeds, garlic, chilli, and all) over the surface of the dhal. The hot fat hits the cold dhal and releases its aromatics in a fragrant burst. Do not stir it in fully; let it sit on top so the dhal carries the flavour layer by layer. Scatter the chopped {{coriander}} of coriander leaves over the top and serve with {{lemon}} wedge of lemon and rice or flatbreads.

items.symptom: Dhal is grainy rather than smooth
items.cause: Lentils were old, or the cook was cut short.
items.fix: Reseal and pressure-cook for another 5 minutes. A short blast with a stick blender restores a smooth purée.

items.symptom: Garlic burnt and turned the tarka bitter
items.cause: Cooked too long or fat was too hot.
items.fix: Discard the burnt tarka and make a fresh one. Garlic in hot fat goes from golden to bitter in 15 seconds; pull it off the heat the moment it colours.
Add another half teaspoon of salt and a squeeze of lemon. Dhal needs salt and acid to taste of itself.
Quick-release, open the lid, skim the foam, and add a tablespoon of oil to suppress further foaming. Reseal and continue.

items.symptom: Dhal sets to a thick paste in the fridge
items.cause: Normal. Lentils absorb water as they cool.
items.fix: Loosen with hot water when reheating. A splash of cream or coconut milk is also good.

content.text: The dhal keeps four days in the fridge and freezes well for three months. Always make a fresh tarka when you serve it; the temper does not survive freezing or reheating.

Tarka dhal is the everyday lentil dish of north Indian home kitchens, the kind cooked twice a day in millions of households. The pressure cooker is so standard that the recipe has shaped itself around the appliance: most modern Punjabi cookbooks assume you have one. The dish belongs in any cook's permanent rotation. It costs almost nothing, takes twenty minutes, and tastes of its ingredients at every stage.
